attributes.title: Personal Crisis
attributes.description: We are exploring how people cope with their personal crisis, especially with AI.

78-2.value: No, no data have been collected for this study yet.

78-7.value:
How do people cope with their personal crisis?
What kind of crisis people are normally facing right now?
Do people use AI to help them cope with personal crisis?
If not, what the constraints of using AI to help cope with personal crisis?

78-9.value:
Please describe an example of a personal crisis you have or might face and how it would make you feel (open-ended)
Which of the following best describes the type of personal crisis you described? (five options: financial, physical, mental, legal, social/interpersonal crisis)
Likelihood of using AI to cope personal crisis (1 = very unlikely; 7 = very likely)
How would people cope with this personal crisis? (three options: Ask a professional for help, Talk about the issue with friends/family, use artificial intelligence tools (ChatGPT, Bing AI, Bard, etc.))
What are the constraints that make you reluctant to use artificial intelligence tools to cope with your personal crisis? (open-ended)
Please rank the likelihood that you would use artificial intelligence tools to assist with the following personal crisis (rank financial, physical, mental, legal, social/interpersonal crisis)
How good do you think ChatGPT's response would be? (1 = Extremely bad; 7 = Extremely good)
How familiar you are with Generative artificial intelligence tools such as ChatGPT? (1 = Not familiar at all; 7 = very familiar)

78-11.value: Participants will not be assigned to any treatment.

78-13.value: We will use some descriptive analyses to explore the pattern in the data.

78-17.value: We aim to recruit 300 participants from a university subject pool. Any deviations from this goal will be due to the subject pool and outside our control.

attributes.title: Coping with crisis
attributes.description: This study will examine how escapism can serve as a coping mechanisms for people in times of crisis. The aim is to explore the relationship between people’s concerns about different crises and their emotional states and how this can be explained by two dimensions of escapism.

q2.value:
H1: Issue salience will have a negative effect on emotions.
H2: Issue salience will have a positive effect on both dimensions of escapism. 
H3: Escapism will mediate the effect of issue salience on emotions.

q3.value: Observational Study - Data is collected from study subjects that are not randomly assigned to a treatment. This includes surveys, “natural experiments,” and regression discontinuity designs.

value: No blinding is involved in this study.

q5.value: No blinding is involved in this study.

question.value: For this study, survey data is used that is collected as part of the ASCoR 25-year collaborative survey. Details about this study (including the study design) are explained in detail here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

q7.value: For this study, survey data is used that is collected as part of the ASCoR 25-year collaborative survey. Details about this study (including the randomization) are explained in detail here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

q8.value: Registration prior to any human observation of the data

q9.value: The data is currently being collected by a third party and has not been shared with anyone. Information about the third party that collected the data can be found here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

question.value: For this study, survey data is used that is collected as part of the ASCoR 25-year collaborative survey. Details about this study (including the data collection procedure) are explained in detail here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

q11.value: For this study, survey data is used that is collected as part of the ASCoR 25-year collaborative survey. Details about this study (including the sample size) are explained in detail here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

question.value:
For this study, survey data is used that is collected as part of the ASCoR 25-year collaborative survey. Details about this study (including the complete questionnaire) are explained in detail here: https://osf.io/d958h/?view_only=bcff911460c540bd818c2ee54e0eda19.

From the survey, the following variables are used:
•	Demographics
•	Issue salience (societal)
•	Issue salience (personal)
•	Problem-solving (= escapism)
•	Emotions
•	Media use (exploratory)

The hypotheses are tested using Hayes' process Model 4. Issue salience (societal) and issue salience (personal) are included as IVs, the two dimensions of escapism (problem-solving variable) are included as mediators, and emotional state is included as DV. Demographics are included as covariates.

registration_responses.q2:
H1: Issue salience will have a negative effect on emotions.
H2: Issue salience will have a positive effect on both dimensions of escapism. 
H3: Escapism will mediate the effect of issue salience on emotions.

attributes.title: Whiteness in Crisis?
attributes.description: Whiteness in Crisis is an in-depth interview project of White southerners that examines how they make sense of race and region against the backdrop of the twenty-first century's extant crises. This research was made possible by grants from the National Science Foundation (award #2115147) and the Russell Sage Foundation (award # G-2101-30264).

attributes.title: CRISIS PSYCHOLOGY
attributes.description:
Crisis Psychology is a comprehensive, field-driven work 
that addresses one of the most urgent gaps in modern 
psychological science: how the human mind functions 
and collapses during acute crisis—and how it can be 
protected in real time.
In an era marked by recurring disasters, legal shocks, 
medical emergencies, institutional pressure, digital panic, 
and social instability, traditional psychology—designed for 
reflective, stable environments—has proven insufficient. 
This book introduces Crisis Psychology as a distinct, 
applied discipline focused on immediate psychological 
stabilization, ethical containment, and prevention of 
irreversible harm when judgment, autonomy, and coping 
mechanisms break down.
Written with exceptional clarity and authority, this book 
integrates psychology with law, medicine, disaster 
management, human rights, and governance, making it 
uniquely multidisciplinary. It moves beyond theory to 
present operational frameworks, protocol-based 
interventions, and institution-ready models that can be 
directly applied by professionals working under extreme 
pressure.
